title: Web Crawler - System Design
description: A step-by-step walkthrough of designing a scalable web crawler for search indexing: seed discovery, URL scheduling, polite fetching, duplicate suppression, freshness, and distributed operation.
capacityDiagram: Frontier[[URL Frontier\nhundreds of millions of URLs]]

functional: Start from seed URLs, download HTML pages, extract links, and keep discovering new pages.
Support search-engine indexing as the primary use case.
Fetch HTML content only; other content types such as images, videos, and PDFs are out of scope for this version.
Store crawled HTML content for up to 5 years.
Discover newly added pages and revisit changed pages.
Ignore pages whose content is duplicate of content already seen.
nonFunctional: Scale to roughly 1 billion pages per month: about 400 pages/sec on average and 800 pages/sec at peak.
Be robust against malformed HTML, timeouts, redirects, server errors, crawler traps, and malicious links.
Be polite: respect robots.txt and avoid sending too many requests to the same host in a short interval.
Be extensible so new content types or extraction rules can be added without redesigning the crawler.
Keep the fetch pipeline observable with crawl status, error reasons, backlog size, and per-host rate metrics.

capacity.label: Pages crawled
capacity.note: Problem scope from the interview input.

capacity.label: Average page size
capacity.note: HTML only; excludes images, videos, and PDFs.

capacity.label: 5-year archive
capacity.note: 500 TB x 12 months x 5 years before compression and dedupe.

capacity.label: Fetch bandwidth
capacity.note: 400 pages/sec x 500 KB; peak roughly 3.2 Gbps.

capacity.label: Frontier size
capacity.note: Large enough to require durable queues and partitioning.

api.description: Add seed URLs or a scoped crawl campaign. This is an operator/control-plane API, not a public web product API.
Fetch crawl metadata and archive pointers for a known URL.
Inspect crawl backlog, per-host readiness, politeness delays, and failed URL counts.
diagram: participant H as Host Policy Store
API->>F: read queue depth by shard

api.description: Ask the scheduler to revisit a URL or host sooner than its normal freshness policy.
diagram: participant H as Host Policy Store
S->>H: check robots and host delay
S->>F: enqueue URL with due time

dataModel.name: urls
dataModel.note: Canonical URL registry and scheduling state. Sharded by url_hash.

fields.name: status
fields.note: new | queued | fetched | failed | blocked | duplicate

dataModel.name: hosts
dataModel.note: Per-host crawling rules, robots.txt cache, and politeness throttle.

dataModel.name: page_versions
dataModel.note: Append-only records for fetched HTML versions. The HTML body itself lives in object storage.

dataModel.name: crawl_attempts
dataModel.note: Operational history for debugging, retry policy, and metrics.

dataModel.name: outlinks
dataModel.note: Discovered graph edges. Can be sampled or TTL-retained if the full graph is too large.

functional.requirement: Start from seed URLs, download pages, extract links, and keep discovering new pages.
functional.how: Seeds enter the URL Frontier; downloader workers fetch ready URLs; parser workers extract links; the normalizer and dedupe layer enqueue unseen URLs back into the frontier.
Fetched, non-duplicate HTML is stored in the archive and published to the indexing feed with URL metadata, content hash, and crawl timestamp.
The downloader follows redirects, checks HTTP status and Content-Type, and drops non-HTML responses before storage and parsing.

functional.requirement: Store crawled HTML for up to 5 years.
functional.how: Compressed HTML bodies are written to object storage with version records in metadata storage; duplicate bodies reuse fingerprints instead of storing repeated payloads.

functional.requirement: Discover newly added and changed pages.
functional.how: The freshness scheduler computes next_fetch_after from page importance, historical change rate, cache validators, and manual recrawl requests.
Content is normalized and hashed after fetch. Already-seen fingerprints are marked duplicate, not emitted as new documents to the index.

nonFunctional.requirement: Scale to roughly 1 billion pages per month.
nonFunctional.how: The final architecture partitions the frontier, URL store, fingerprint index, and worker pools by hash so capacity grows by adding shards and workers.

nonFunctional.requirement: Be robust against bad HTML, timeouts, redirects, traps, and malicious links.
nonFunctional.how: Workers isolate fetch failures into crawl_attempts, enforce size/time limits, cap redirect chains, normalize URLs, and use trap filters before enqueueing new work.
The host policy store caches robots.txt and per-host next_allowed_at. The scheduler only releases URLs when host-level delay constraints allow another fetch.

nonFunctional.requirement: Be extensible for future content types.
nonFunctional.how: The pipeline separates fetching, type filtering, parsing, storage, and indexing emission, so new parsers can be added behind the same worker interfaces.
Crawl attempts, frontier stats, per-host delay state, worker metrics, and indexing-feed lag expose bottlenecks and error patterns.

followUps: How would you detect and escape crawler traps such as infinite calendars or session-id URLs?
How would you choose recrawl frequency for high-change news sites versus mostly static pages?
How would you implement near-duplicate detection beyond exact content hashing?
How would you partition the URL frontier so a single hot domain cannot starve other hosts?
How would you recover from a queue shard failure without losing pending URLs?
How would you change the design to crawl PDFs and images later?
How would you prevent the crawler from fetching private IP ranges or internal metadata endpoints?
How would you keep a 30 PB archive cost-effective over 5 years?
What metrics should page the on-call engineer first?

steps.title: 1. Basic Crawler Loop
steps.description: Start with the simplest model: a small seed list feeds one crawler process. The crawler downloads a page, extracts links from the HTML, and appends new links to an in-memory list. This proves the algorithm, but it is not durable, polite, or scalable.
steps.decisionPrompt: Define the smallest correct crawl loop: how does one worker fetch a page, extract links, remember visited URLs, and stop immediate cycles?

concepts.definition: An initial URL used to start crawling a site or topic.
concepts.whyItMatters: Seeds determine the first frontier and strongly influence crawl coverage.

concepts.definition: A record of URLs already discovered or fetched.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prevents immediate loops and repeated work.

options.name: In-memory queue (default)
pros: Fastest way to explain and test the crawl loop
No external dependencies for a prototype
Easy to reason about ordering and duplicate checks
cons: Loses pending URLs on restart
Cannot support multiple workers safely
No place to persist retry or priority state

options.name: Local durable queue
pros: Survives process restarts during early testing
Introduces the durable-work concept before distributed queues
Makes retry and resume behavior visible
cons: Still a single-machine bottleneck
Queue locking limits concurrency
Not enough for host-level politeness or web-scale partitioning

deepDives.title: Basic algorithm
points: Given seed URLs, download each page.
Extract URLs from the downloaded HTML.
Add unseen URLs to the list and repeat.

deepDives.title: Why this is only a starting point
points: The in-memory queue is lost on restart.
One process cannot reach the required 400 pages/sec average rate reliably.
It can accidentally hammer one host because there is no per-host scheduling.

bottlenecks.issue: In-memory queue and seen set are not durable.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Move discovered URLs into a persistent URL frontier and URL store.

bottlenecks.issue: No politeness enforcement.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Introduce per-host scheduling and robots.txt handling before scaling fetch workers.

talkingPoints: Clarify that this design is useful only to explain the crawl loop.
State that large-scale crawling is mostly scheduling, dedupe, and operational control rather than just HTTP fetching.

flows.name: Single-process crawl
flows.note: The first version downloads one page and immediately feeds newly extracted links back into the local queue.

flows.name: Prototype duplicate skip
flows.note: Even the prototype needs a seen set; otherwise one cycle of links can loop forever.

steps.title: 2. Add a Durable URL Frontier
steps.description: Replace the in-memory list with a durable URL Frontier. The frontier tracks pending URLs, priority, due time, and retry state. A separate URL Store remembers canonical URLs that have already been seen, preventing repeated enqueueing.
steps.decisionPrompt: Choose the durable URL frontier contract: what state is stored per URL, how are leases and retries handled, and how does crawl progress survive crashes?

concepts.definition: The durable queue or scheduler that owns pending URLs and their crawl state.
concepts.whyItMatters: Crawler progress must survive worker crashes and restarts.

concepts.definition: A temporary claim that lets one worker fetch a URL before another worker can take it.
concepts.whyItMatters: Leases prevent duplicate fetches while allowing retry after worker failure.

options.name: Priority frontier (default)
pros: Directly models priority, due time, retries, and leases
Good fit for crawl scheduling and recrawl freshness
Easy for workers to lease ready URLs
cons: Queue shards must be carefully partitioned
Large delayed queues require custom storage strategy
Operationally more complex than a simple append-only log

options.name: Log-backed frontier
pros: Append-only ingestion has high throughput
Replay makes recovery and auditing straightforward
Compaction can remove obsolete duplicate URL events
cons: Due-time scheduling and priority reads are awkward on a plain log
Workers can waste effort scanning events that are not ready
Requires an extra materialized scheduler view

deepDives.title: Frontier responsibilities
points: Order URLs by priority and next_fetch_after.
Persist pending work so a worker crash does not lose URLs.
Support retries with backoff and dead-letter handling for repeated failures.

deepDives.title: URL canonicalization
points: Normalize scheme and host case, remove URL fragments, sort safe query parameters, and resolve relative links.
Reject invalid schemes such as mailto:, javascript:, and data:.
Hash canonical_url to create the partition key and dedupe key.

bottlenecks.issue: A single queue partition becomes a throughput and availability bottleneck.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Partition the frontier by URL hash or host hash, then assign worker pools per partition.

bottlenecks.issue: URL dedupe can be expensive at web scale.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Use a durable key-value URL store, optionally fronted by a Bloom filter to reduce read load.

talkingPoints: Separate URL dedupe from content dedupe: two different URLs can have identical HTML.
The frontier is a scheduler, not just a FIFO queue.

flows.name: Seed ingestion
flows.note: Seeds are normalized and deduped before entering the frontier.

flows.name: Lease timeout and retry
flows.note: A durable frontier can recover leased URLs when a worker crashes mid-fetch.
diagram: F-->>W2: same URL with later due time

steps.title: 3. Scale Fetching with Downloader Workers
steps.description: Add a pool of stateless downloader workers. Workers lease ready URLs from the frontier, resolve DNS through a shared resolver cache, enforce connection and response-size limits, follow bounded redirects, and drop non-HTML responses.
steps.decisionPrompt: Scale fetching with workers without being rude or wasteful: how are hosts scheduled, DNS and redirects handled, and what limits protect the crawler?

concepts.definition: A stateless process that leases URLs and performs network fetches.
concepts.whyItMatters: Fetch throughput scales by adding workers, not by making one crawler loop larger.

concepts.definition: A cache of hostname to IP lookups shared by crawler workers.
concepts.whyItMatters: Repeated DNS lookups can dominate latency and overload resolvers at crawl scale.

options.name: Central worker pool (default)
pros: Simple autoscaling model
Workers are stateless and interchangeable
Shared resolver cache reduces repeated DNS lookups
cons: A regional outage can stop all fetching
Egress bandwidth is concentrated in one location
Long-distance fetches can add latency

options.name: Regional worker pools
pros: Scales egress bandwidth across regions
Can fetch closer to origin servers
Region failure does not stop the whole crawl
cons: Requires global lease coordination
Duplicate fetches are easier to introduce
Per-host politeness must still be globally enforced

deepDives.title: Downloader guardrails
points: Set connect, read, and total request timeouts.
Cap response size and redirect depth.
Only pass HTTP 200 HTML responses to the parser; record other outcomes as crawl attempts.

deepDives.title: DNS strategy
points: A resolver cache avoids repeated DNS lookups for popular hosts.
Respect DNS TTLs and handle DNS failures as retryable crawl attempts.
Protect workers from slow resolver calls with circuit breakers and timeouts.

bottlenecks.issue: Outbound bandwidth and connection counts cap crawl rate.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Scale downloader pools horizontally and place them close to egress capacity.

bottlenecks.issue: Slow or malicious servers tie up worker slots.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Aggressive timeouts, byte limits, redirect caps, and per-host failure backoff.

bottlenecks.issue: DNS resolver overload adds latency to every fetch.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Shared resolver cache and batching popular host lookups.

talkingPoints: At 400 pages/sec and 500 KB average page size, fetch bandwidth is roughly 1.6 Gbps before protocol overhead.
Treat network failures as normal data, not exceptional crashes.

flows.name: Fetch ready URL
flows.note: Workers lease URLs from the frontier and record every outcome for retry policy and observability.
diagram: W->>F: retry later or mark failed

flows.name: Redirect and content-type guard
flows.note: The downloader follows only bounded redirects and drops responses outside the HTML scope.
diagram: else redirect loop or too many hops
else PDF, image, video, or huge body

steps.title: 4. Parse HTML and Deduplicate URLs
steps.description: Parser workers extract links, canonicalize them, filter unsafe or out-of-scope URLs, and write graph edges. Only URLs not already present in the URL Store are sent back to the frontier. This prevents the crawler from revisiting the same URL endlessly.
steps.decisionPrompt: Design URL discovery and canonicalization: when do you normalize, deduplicate, and enqueue links so equivalent URLs do not explode the frontier?

concepts.definition: A normalized URL form used to compare equivalent links.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without canonicalization, the crawler may treat the same page as many URLs.

concepts.definition: A memory-efficient probabilistic set that can say an item is probably present.
concepts.whyItMatters: It reduces duplicate enqueue attempts at very large URL volumes, with acceptable false positives in some paths.

options.name: Durable URL store (default)
pros: Exact dedupe with no false positives
Simple correctness story for canonical URLs
Works well with batch put-if-absent writes
cons: Every candidate URL can become a storage lookup
Needs sharding and write batching at scale
Higher latency than in-memory probabilistic checks

options.name: Bloom filter + URL store
pros: Cuts read load for URLs that are obviously already known
Keeps exactness by confirming misses in the durable store
Useful when extracted-link volume is much larger than fetch volume
cons: False positives can hide new URLs if used as the only check
Filter rebuilds and rotation add operational complexity
Still needs the durable URL Store as source of truth

deepDives.title: Trap and safety filters
points: Reject unsupported schemes, malformed URLs, private IP ranges, and extremely long URLs.
Detect patterns that create infinite spaces, such as calendar next links or session IDs.
Limit per-page and per-host discovered URL fanout so one page cannot flood the frontier.

deepDives.title: HTML parsing reality
points: The web contains broken markup, wrong encodings, and duplicate links.
Use a tolerant parser and normalize relative links against the fetched page URL.
Parsing failures should mark the page as fetched-but-unparsed, not crash the worker.

bottlenecks.issue: Pages with thousands of links can overload the frontier.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Apply per-page link caps and priority scoring before enqueueing.

bottlenecks.issue: URL Store lookup volume grows with every extracted link.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Batch put-if-absent calls and use a Bloom filter to skip obvious repeats.

talkingPoints: This step is about URL uniqueness; duplicate content is handled after HTML body hashing.
URL normalization rules must be conservative because over-normalization can merge distinct pages.

flows.name: Extract and enqueue links
flows.note: Only canonical URLs that pass filters and are absent from the URL Store re-enter the frontier.

flows.name: Trap or duplicate rejected
flows.note: Bad, cyclic, or already-known URLs are stopped before they expand the frontier.

steps.title: 5. Store HTML and Suppress Duplicate Content
steps.description: Fetched HTML must be retained for up to 5 years, but pages with duplicate content should be ignored by the indexing pipeline. Normalize the HTML body, compute a content fingerprint, store only unique versions, and emit unique documents to the search indexing pipeline.
steps.decisionPrompt: Choose what fetched content to persist and index: how do you separate raw HTML, parsed metadata, and duplicate content fingerprints?

concepts.definition: A hash or signature of fetched page content.
concepts.whyItMatters: Fingerprints detect duplicate or near-duplicate pages even when URLs differ.

concepts.definition: Storage for the fetched HTML or response body before downstream indexing.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets parsing, indexing, and debugging replay the original crawl result.

options.name: Exact hash dedupe (default)
pros: Deterministic and easy to explain
No false positives for distinct HTML bodies
Cheap key-value lookup by content_hash
cons: Misses near-duplicates with small template or ad changes
Requires careful normalization to avoid noisy hashes
Does not cluster mirrors with lightly rewritten content

options.name: Exact + near-duplicate
pros: Suppresses mirrored pages and template-level duplicates
Improves index quality for search use cases
Can be introduced after exact hashing as a second-stage scorer
cons: False positives can remove distinct useful pages
More CPU-heavy than exact hashing
Needs threshold tuning and quality evaluation

deepDives.title: Exact duplicate detection
points: Normalize volatile markup where safe, then compute a SHA-256 hash of the HTML body.
If the hash already exists, mark this URL version as duplicate and avoid emitting a new indexing document.
Keep the URL metadata even for duplicates so crawl coverage remains visible.

deepDives.title: Storage layout
points: Compress HTML before writing to object storage.
Partition archive keys by content hash prefix or crawl date to avoid hot object-store prefixes.
Keep metadata in a queryable store; keep large HTML bodies in cheaper object storage.

bottlenecks.issue: 30 PB raw retention is expensive.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Use compression, duplicate suppression, lifecycle tiers, and selective retention policies.

bottlenecks.issue: Fingerprint index becomes a high-QPS key-value workload.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Shard by content_hash and cache recently seen fingerprints.

bottlenecks.issue: Indexer lag hides fresh pages from search.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Expose Indexer Feed lag and scale downstream consumers independently.

talkingPoints: Distinguish archive retention from index freshness: the archive can store versions while the index only consumes unique/current content.
Near-duplicate detection such as SimHash can be added later, but exact hashing is the first interview-safe answer.

flows.name: Archive unique HTML
flows.note: Duplicate bodies are not emitted as new indexing documents.

flows.name: Near-duplicate scoring
flows.note: A second-stage scorer can suppress pages that are not byte-identical but are redundant for the index.
diagram: N-->>F: near duplicate of known page

steps.title: 6. Enforce Robots.txt and Politeness
steps.description: A scalable crawler must not overwhelm websites. Introduce a Host Policy Store that caches robots.txt, crawl-delay rules, and next_allowed_at per host. The frontier only releases a URL when both global capacity and host-level politeness allow it.
steps.decisionPrompt: Place the robots.txt and politeness gate: how does a URL move from eligible to fetchable while respecting per-host delay and disallow rules?

concepts.definition: A site-provided policy file that tells crawlers which paths they may fetch.
concepts.whyItMatters: Respecting it is a core correctness and trust requirement for crawlers.

concepts.definition: A minimum wait time between requests to the same host.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prevents the crawler from overwhelming websites.

options.name: Central host policy (default)
pros: Strong per-host politeness because all leases consult one logical state
Simple robots.txt cache ownership
Clear place to track next_allowed_at and host failure score
cons: Host policy store becomes a high-QPS dependency
Needs careful partitioning by host
Scheduler latency can block worker utilization

options.name: Per-host ready queues
pros: Politeness is encoded directly in queue release times
Large delayed hosts do not crowd out ready hosts
Natural fit for fairness across domains
cons: Millions of host queues require compact metadata
Hot hosts need special splitting without breaking delay guarantees
Global priority is harder to optimize

deepDives.title: Politeness mechanics
points: Group URLs by host and release at most one request per host per configured delay window.
Honor robots.txt disallow rules and crawl-delay where available.
Use exponential backoff when a host returns repeated 429, 503, timeout, or connection errors.

deepDives.title: Scheduling tradeoff
points: A global priority queue maximizes freshness but can cluster requests on popular hosts.
Per-host queues preserve politeness and fairness.
The practical design combines per-host queues with a global ready-time heap.

bottlenecks.issue: Popular hosts create large delayed queues.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Store per-host queues separately and release only due items into worker-visible partitions.

bottlenecks.issue: Robots.txt fetch failures block large sites.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Cache previous rules, use short retry backoff, and fail closed or conservative for unknown rules.

bottlenecks.issue: Clock skew between schedulers can violate delay windows.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Centralize per-host next_allowed_at updates or use compare-and-set leases.

talkingPoints: Politeness is a hard requirement, not an optimization.
Scaling workers without host-aware scheduling makes the crawler more dangerous, not better.

flows.name: Polite lease
flows.note: The scheduler checks host policy before handing a URL to a downloader.
diagram: participant H as Host Policy Store

flows.name: Host backoff after 429
flows.note: Host feedback updates the same policy state used by future leases.
diagram: participant P as Host Policy Store

steps.title: 7. Recrawl for Freshness
steps.description: The crawler must handle newly added and edited pages. Add a freshness scheduler that revisits known URLs based on importance, historical change rate, previous HTTP validators, and manual recrawl requests. Conditional GETs reduce bandwidth when pages have not changed.
steps.decisionPrompt: Design recrawl scheduling: which signals decide next_fetch_after, and how do you balance freshness against crawl budget and host politeness?

concepts.definition: How current the stored copy of a page is compared with the live web page.
concepts.whyItMatters: Important or frequently changing pages need recrawls sooner than stable pages.

concepts.definition: The limited fetch capacity allocated to revisiting known URLs.
concepts.whyItMatters: Every recrawl competes with discovering new pages and with politeness constraints.

options.name: Adaptive recrawl (default)
pros: Uses observed change history to spend crawl budget where it matters
Balances discovery and freshness explicitly
Works even when sites provide no external change signals
cons: Can be slow to notice sudden changes on historically stable pages
Needs tuning by page importance and host behavior
Consumes capacity on pages that may not have changed

options.name: Signal-assisted recrawl
pros: Sitemaps, feeds, and manual signals can surface new or changed pages quickly
Reduces wasted recrawls on stable pages
Good fit for high-value publishers and news sources
cons: Signals are incomplete and not uniformly available
Bad or spammy feeds can poison priority
Still needs adaptive fallback for the rest of the web
diagram: Signals[/Sitemaps RSS Change Feeds Manual Recrawl/]

deepDives.title: Recrawl policy
points: Frequently changing or high-importance pages get shorter recrawl intervals.
Stable pages get longer intervals to preserve capacity.
HTTP 304 responses update last_checked_at without writing a new page version.

deepDives.title: Freshness vs coverage
points: Capacity spent on recrawling cannot be spent discovering brand-new pages.
The scheduler should reserve separate budgets for discovery and freshness.
Manual recrawl requests enter with higher priority but still obey politeness.

bottlenecks.issue: Over-recrawling stable pages wastes bandwidth and worker slots.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Use adaptive intervals driven by observed change frequency.

bottlenecks.issue: Under-recrawling important pages makes the search index stale.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Weight recrawl priority by page importance and update velocity.

talkingPoints: Freshness is an explicit scheduling problem, not something solved by the downloader.
Use ETag and Last-Modified when hosts provide them, but do not rely on them being correct everywhere.

flows.name: Conditional recrawl
flows.note: Known pages can be revisited cheaply when HTTP validators are available.
diagram: S->>U: find URLs due for recrawl

flows.name: Manual priority recrawl
flows.note: Operator-triggered recrawls raise priority but still pass through the same politeness gate.
diagram: participant H as Host Policy Store
S->>H: check host delay and robots state
S->>F: enqueue URL with priority and due time

steps.title: 8. Distributed Crawler at Web Scale
steps.description: At the target scale, every major component is partitioned: the frontier, URL Store, fingerprint index, parser queue, downloader workers, and archive. Workers are stateless and horizontally scalable; durable stores own the crawl state. Metrics and backpressure keep the system controllable.
steps.decisionPrompt: Partition the crawler at web scale: choose ownership for hosts, frontier shards, fetch workers, and storage so duplicates and cross-region contention stay bounded.

concepts.definition: Assign all URLs for a host to a specific shard or scheduler owner.
concepts.whyItMatters: It keeps politeness decisions local and avoids cross-shard coordination for one site.

concepts.definition: A component that maps URLs or hosts to the partition responsible for them.
concepts.whyItMatters: Routing determines where work, dedupe state, and metrics are updated.

options.name: Hash-sharded pipeline (default)
pros: Evenly distributes URL and content-fingerprint workloads
Straightforward to add partitions as crawl volume grows
Works well for stateless worker pools
cons: Per-host politeness must coordinate across shards
Hot hosts can still concentrate delayed work
Resharding requires careful migration of pending leases

options.name: Host-sharded frontier
pros: Keeps per-host ordering and politeness local to one shard
Reduces cross-shard coordination for next_allowed_at
Makes host-level debugging easier
cons: Host popularity creates uneven shard load
Hot-host splitting is harder without violating politeness
URL Store and Fingerprint Index may still need independent hash sharding

deepDives.title: Partitioning
points: Shard URL Store by url_hash for even distribution.
Shard Host Policy by host so politeness updates are serialized per host.
Shard the Fingerprint Index by content_hash to distribute duplicate checks.

deepDives.title: Backpressure
points: If parser lag grows, downloader workers slow down or stop leasing new URLs.
If archive writes slow down, unique-content processing queues apply backpressure.
If a host error rate spikes, its host policy delays future fetches.

deepDives.title: Observability
points: Track frontier depth, ready URLs, delayed URLs, fetch QPS, success rate, bytes/sec, parser lag, archive write latency, duplicate ratio, and index feed lag.
Expose per-host metrics to detect accidental hammering.
Record crawl_attempts so operators can debug why a URL was not indexed.

bottlenecks.issue: A shard can become hot because many URLs belong to one host or one campaign.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Use host-aware scheduling plus enough partitions; split hot host queues if needed while preserving per-host delay.

bottlenecks.issue: Large queue replays after outage can violate politeness.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Rehydrate delayed queues through the scheduler rather than dumping all pending URLs directly to workers.

bottlenecks.issue: Downstream indexer or archive outage causes unbounded backlog.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Apply backpressure, preserve durable queues, and shed low-priority recrawls first.

talkingPoints: At this point the crawler is a distributed data pipeline with HTTP at the edge.
The critical correctness properties are dedupe, durability of pending work, and politeness.

flows.name: Distributed crawl path
flows.note: The full pipeline keeps workers stateless and pushes state into partitioned durable systems.
diagram: participant H as Host Policy Store

flows.name: Backpressure control
flows.note: The control loop slows leasing before archive or index lag turns into an unbounded backlog.
diagram: M->>C: alert on lag and latency
C->>F: reduce lease budget for low-priority work

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end crawler architecture: seed ingestion, durable frontier, host-aware scheduling, robots and politeness enforcement, downloader workers, URL parsing and dedupe, content fingerprinting, archival storage, freshness scheduling, distributed ownership, and observability.

options.name: Host-owned distributed crawler
pros: Politeness decisions stay local to host ownership
Frontier and workers scale horizontally
Dedupe and freshness are explicit
Backpressure can be applied per host or shard
cons: More routing and ownership machinery
Hot hosts can still require special handling
Distributed frontier operations need careful lease semantics

options.name: Single-region frontier with worker pool
pros: Simpler deployment and debugging
Enough for a focused or smaller crawler
Still supports durable leases and parsing pipeline
cons: Lower global scale ceiling
Frontier can become a central bottleneck
Politeness and host state are harder to partition later
